description: Pharmaceutical sciences, pharmacology, drug interactions, clinical pharmacy

exam_modes.system_persona: You are an expert NAPLEX exam question writer with deep knowledge of pharmaceutical sciences, pharmacokinetics, drug interactions, and clinical pharmacy practice. You write questions that test a pharmacist's ability to ensure safe and effective medication therapy.

study_guide_context: This content covers pharmaceutical and pharmacological topics. Ensure questions use precise pharmacological terminology and test understanding of drug mechanisms, pharmacokinetics, drug interactions, dosing calculations, and clinical pharmacy applications.

example_questions.stem: A 65-year-old patient stabilized on warfarin (INR 2.5) is prescribed fluconazole for a fungal infection. What is the most appropriate pharmacist recommendation?
options: Reduce warfarin dose by 25-50% and monitor INR within 3-5 days
No dose adjustment needed — monitor only if symptoms occur
Switch to a direct oral anticoagulant for the duration of antifungal therapy
example_questions.rationale: Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, the primary enzyme metabolizing S-warfarin, causing INR elevation and increased bleeding risk. A proactive warfarin dose reduction of 25-50% with close INR monitoring is standard practice. Discontinuation risks thromboembolism; switching anticoagulants introduces unnecessary complexity.

example_questions.stem: A pharmacist is reviewing medications for a patient with Stage 4 chronic kidney disease (GFR 18 mL/min). Which medications require dose adjustment or discontinuation? (Select all that apply)
example_questions.rationale: Metformin is contraindicated when GFR < 30 due to lactic acidosis risk. Gabapentin is renally cleared and requires dose reduction. Enoxaparin accumulates in renal impairment, increasing bleeding risk. Lisinopril may be continued with monitoring. Atorvastatin is hepatically metabolized and requires no renal adjustment.
